cards.heading: STRESS & STRAIN FUNDAMENTALS

formulas.label: NORMAL STRESS
formulas.text: σ = P/A  (axial force / cross-sectional area)

formulas.label: NORMAL STRAIN
formulas.text: ε = δL/L  (change in length / original length)

formulas.label: HOOKE'S LAW
formulas.text: σ = E·ε  (within elastic limit)

points: Shear stress: τ = V/A; Shear strain: γ = τ/G
Poisson's ratio: μ = −(lateral strain)/(axial strain)
For composite/stepped bars: total δ = Σ(PL/AE) for each section
cards.alert: For composite/stepped bars: total δ = Σ(PL/AE) for each section

cards.heading: ELASTIC CONSTANTS & RELATIONS

formulas.label: FOUR ELASTIC CONSTANTS
formulas.text: E (Young's), G/C (Shear/Rigidity), K (Bulk), μ/ν (Poisson's ratio)

cards.tip: Memorize: G = E/[2(1+μ)] and K = E/[3(1−2μ)]

cards.heading: THERMAL STRESS & STRESS-STRAIN CURVE

formulas.label: THERMAL STRESS (FREE EXPANSION RESTRAINED)
formulas.text: σ_th = αEΔT  |  ε_th = αΔT  |  Force = αEAΔT

formulas.label: α values
formulas.text: Steel: 12×10⁻⁶/°C, Concrete: 10–12×10⁻⁶/°C, Aluminium: 23×10⁻⁶/°C

points: Key points on stress-strain curve: Proportional limit → Elastic limit → Upper yield point → Lower yield point → Ultimate stress → Breaking stress
Strain hardening occurs between lower yield point and ultimate stress
Necking occurs after ultimate stress before fracture
cards.alert: Thermal stress only develops if expansion is RESTRAINED. Free expansion produces strain but no stress.

cards.heading: SF & BM FUNDAMENTAL RELATIONS

formulas.label: DIFFERENTIAL RELATIONS
formulas.text: w = −dV/dx  |  V = dM/dx  |  w = −d²M/dx²

points: Point load → step change in SFD; no change in BMD shape
UDL → linear change in SFD; parabolic change in BMD
Point moment → step change in BMD; no change in SFD
Max BM occurs where SF = 0 or changes sign
Point of contraflexure: where BM = 0 (changes sign)
cards.alert: ⚠ Sign convention: Sagging is positive in most Indian textbooks (Khurmi, Punmia)

cards.heading: STANDARD CASES — MAX BM & SF

rows: Cantilever, point load W at free end
WL/8 at centre; WL/8 at ends
wL²/12 at ends; wL²/24 at centre

cards.tip: For SS UVL: max BM at x = L/√3 from lighter end

cards.heading: OVERHANGING BEAM & ECCENTRIC LOAD

formulas.label: SS BEAM WITH ECCENTRIC POINT LOAD
formulas.text: P at distance 'a' from A (b = L−a): R_A = Pb/L, R_B = Pa/L. Max BM under load = Pab/L

points: Overhanging beam: hogging BM at support; possible sagging in span → point of contraflexure exists
Concentrated couple M₀: causes sudden jump of M₀ in BMD; SFD unchanged
cards.tip: For SS beam with two equal loads P at L/3 each: max BM = PL/3 between loads (constant region)

cards.heading: EULER-BERNOULLI BENDING EQUATION

formulas.label: FUNDAMENTAL EQUATION
formulas.text: M/I = σ/y = E/R

formulas.label: MAX BENDING STRESS
formulas.text: σ_max = M·y_max/I = M/Z  (Z = I/y_max = Section modulus)

points: Assumptions: Plane sections remain plane; material is homogeneous, isotropic, follows Hooke's law
Bending stress varies linearly from NA (zero) to extreme fibre (maximum)
Tension on one side, compression on other side
cards.alert: Assumptions: Plane sections remain plane; material is homogeneous, isotropic, follows Hooke's law; small deflection; pure bending.

cards.heading: SECTION MODULUS — STANDARD SECTIONS
points: For same area: Hollow circle has more Z than solid (more efficient in bending)
Efficiency comparison (same depth, same area): I-section > T-section > Rectangle > Circle
cards.alert: For asymmetric sections (like T): two Z values, use smaller for safety.

cards.heading: BEAM OF UNIFORM STRENGTH & FLITCHED BEAM
points: Beam of uniform strength: section varies so that σ_max = M(x)/Z(x) = constant throughout
Cantilever with UDL: depth varies parabolically for uniform strength
Flitched beam: composite material beam (e.g., timber + steel)
Equate strain at common interface: ε₁ = ε₂ → σ₁/E₁ = σ₂/E₂
Use modular ratio m = E_steel/E_wood for transformed section
cards.tip: For flitched beam: M = M₁ + M₂ where moments are shared based on EI ratio

cards.heading: SHEAR STRESS FORMULA

formulas.label: TRANSVERSE SHEAR STRESS
formulas.text: τ = VQ/(Ib) = V·Aȳ/(Ib)

points: V = shear force at section, Q = first moment of area above point about NA, I = second moment of area, b = width at point
Shear stress distribution is parabolic for rectangular section
Shear stress is ZERO at extreme fibres (top/bottom)
Shear stress is MAXIMUM at the neutral axis (for solid sections)
cards.alert: Shear stress is ZERO at extreme fibres and MAXIMUM at the neutral axis for solid sections.

cards.heading: MAX SHEAR STRESS / AVG RATIOS
cards.tip: For I-section: most shear is carried by the WEB (~95%), most BM by FLANGES

cards.heading: SHEAR FLOW & DISTRIBUTION

formulas.label: SHEAR FLOW q
formulas.text: q = τ × b = VQ/I  (force per unit length)

points: Shear flow is useful for designing connections (rivets, bolts, nails) in built-up sections
In I-section: sudden jump in shear stress at flange-web junction due to width change
For triangular section: Max τ at mid-height (NOT at NA which is at h/3 from base)
For thin-walled tubes: τ varies inversely with thickness t; q = τ·t = constant for constant V
cards.alert: For thin-walled tubes: q = τ·t = constant for constant V (shear flow is uniform).

cards.heading: TORSION FUNDAMENTAL EQUATION

formulas.label: TORSION EQUATION
formulas.text: T/J = τ/r = Gθ/L

formulas.label: MAX SHEAR STRESS (at outer surface)
formulas.text: τ_max = Tr_max/J = T/Z_p  (Z_p = J/r_max = polar section modulus)

formulas.label: TWIST ANGLE
formulas.text: θ = TL/(GJ)  (radians)

points: T = applied torque, J = polar moment of inertia, G = modulus of rigidity
Shear stress varies linearly from zero at centre to maximum at outer surface
Assumptions: Circular shaft, plane sections remain plane (NO warping), small twist, linear elastic
cards.alert: For non-circular sections (square, rectangle): plane sections WARP → torsion equation doesn't apply directly. Use St. Venant's theory.

cards.heading: POLAR MOMENT & POLAR SECTION MODULUS
cards.tip: Hollow shaft has higher J/weight than solid → more efficient for torsion. For same outer diameter, J reduces by factor (1−k⁴) where k = d/D.

cards.heading: POWER TRANSMISSION & APPLICATIONS

formulas.label: POWER TRANSMITTED
formulas.text: P = T·ω = T × 2πN/60  (T in N·m, N in rpm, P in Watts)

points: Series shafts: same torque T; total twist = sum of individual twists
Parallel shafts: same twist angle; torques distributed inversely as torsional stiffness (GJ/L)
Combined bending + torsion: shafts subjected to both bending moment M and torque T

cards.heading: BEAM DEFLECTION FUNDAMENTALS

formulas.label: DIFFERENTIAL EQUATION
formulas.text: EI·d²y/dx² = M(x)  |  EI·dy/dx = ∫M dx + C₁  |  EI·y = ∫∫M dx² + C₁x + C₂

points: BCs for simply supported beam: y = 0 at both supports
BCs for fixed end: y = 0 AND dy/dx = 0
BCs for free end: M = 0 AND V = 0
Macaulay's method: handles multiple discontinuous loads using step functions [x−a]ⁿ
cards.tip: For maximum deflection, use dy/dx = 0 (slope = zero point). Max deflection ≠ centre unless loading is symmetric.

cards.heading: STANDARD DEFLECTION FORMULAS

rows: SS beam, central point load W
Cantilever, point load W at free end
0 at all sections of fixed ends

cards.tip: Memorize: SS UDL = 5wL⁴/(384EI), Fixed UDL = wL⁴/(384EI) — fixed beam deflects 5× less!

cards.heading: MOHR'S THEOREMS & CONJUGATE BEAM

formulas.label: MOHR'S THEOREM I (CHANGE IN SLOPE)
formulas.text: θ_B − θ_A = (1/EI) × Area of BMD between A and B

formulas.label: MOHR'S THEOREM II (DEFLECTION)
formulas.text: Distance of B from tangent at A = (1/EI) × Moment of BMD area between A and B about B

points: Conjugate beam method: load conjugate beam with M/EI diagram
In conjugate beam: SF = slope in real beam; BM = deflection in real beam
Support transformations: fixed → free; free → fixed; pin → pin; roller → roller

cards.heading: EULER BUCKLING THEORY

formulas.label: EULER CRITICAL LOAD (BUCKLING LOAD)
formulas.text: P_E = π²EI/L_e²

formulas.label: SLENDERNESS RATIO
formulas.text: λ = L_e/r_min  (r_min = √(I_min/A) = min radius of gyration)

points: Euler's formula valid ONLY for long columns (high λ) where σ_E < σ_yield
Column buckles about axis of MINIMUM moment of inertia
Short column (λ < 32 for steel): fails by crushing
Long column (λ > 120 for steel): fails by buckling
Intermediate (32–120): both effects → use Rankine
cards.alert: ⚠ Euler's formula is valid only for LONG columns. For short/intermediate columns, use Rankine.

cards.heading: EFFECTIVE LENGTH FOR DIFFERENT END CONDITIONS

rows: One end fixed, one end free (cantilever)
One end fixed, one end pinned

cards.tip: Memorize P_E ratio for the four cases: 1/4 : 1 : 2 : 4 (free-pin, pin-pin, fix-pin, fix-fix)

cards.heading: RANKINE'S FORMULA & ECCENTRIC LOADING

formulas.label: RANKINE'S FORMULA (intermediate columns)
formulas.text: P_R = (σ_C × A) / [1 + a(L_e/r)²]  where a = σ_C/(π²E) = Rankine's constant

formulas.label: ECCENTRIC AXIAL LOAD
formulas.text: σ = P/A ± Pe·y/I = P/A (1 ± ey/r²)

formulas.label: KERN (for NO TENSION)
formulas.text: Rectangle: e ≤ d/6 (middle third). Circle: e ≤ d/8 (middle quarter)

cards.tip: 'Middle third rule' — crucial for masonry/concrete design where tension is undesirable

cards.heading: STRAIN ENERGY FORMULAS

formulas.label: GENERAL STRAIN ENERGY
formulas.text: U = (1/2) × σ²/E × Volume  or  U = (1/2) × Force × Deflection (gradually applied load)

rows: Axial (bar of length L, area A, load P)

cards.tip: SI unit of strain energy: Joule (N·m). Density: J/m³.

cards.heading: RESILIENCE & TOUGHNESS

formulas.label: PROOF RESILIENCE
formulas.text: U_proof = σ_e²/(2E) × Volume  (where σ_e = elastic limit stress)

formulas.label: MODULUS OF RESILIENCE
formulas.text: Proof resilience per unit volume = σ_e²/(2E)

points: Toughness = total area under stress-strain curve up to fracture point
High resilience → good for springs, energy-absorbing members
High toughness → good for impact-resistant members
Brittle materials have low toughness despite high strength
cards.alert: High strength + high ductility = high toughness. Brittle materials have low toughness despite high strength.

cards.heading: IMPACT/SUDDEN LOADING

formulas.label: SUDDENLY APPLIED LOAD (no impact)
formulas.text: σ = 2P/A (twice gradual stress)  |  δ = 2δ_static

formulas.label: IMPACT LOAD (falling weight P from height h)

points: Gradually applied load: σ = P/A, δ = PL/(AE)
Suddenly applied load (h = 0): σ = 2P/A, δ = 2δ_static
Impact load (h > 0): even higher stress; depends on h and δ_static
To reduce impact stress: increase volume of absorbing region

cards.heading: BENDING + AXIAL (DIRECT) STRESS

formulas.label: RESULTANT STRESS
formulas.text: σ = P/A ± M·y/I = (Direct) ± (Bending)

formulas.label: KERN BOUNDARIES (for no tension)
formulas.text: Rectangle b×d: e ≤ d/6 along d-direction. Circle d: e ≤ d/8 (radial)

points: Critical for masonry, concrete columns, foundations under inclined loads, retaining walls
Eccentrically loaded column: same as axial + bending case
cards.alert: Critical for masonry, concrete columns, foundations — ensure no tension develops if material is weak in tension.

formulas.label: EQUIVALENT BENDING MOMENT
formulas.text: M_e = (1/2)[M + √(M² + T²)]  (max normal stress theory)

formulas.label: EQUIVALENT TWISTING MOMENT
formulas.text: T_e = √(M² + T²)  (max shear stress theory)

formulas.label: PRINCIPAL STRESSES
formulas.text: σ₁,₂ = σ/2 ± √[(σ/2)² + τ²]  where σ = 32M/(πd³), τ = 16T/(πd³)

cards.heading: THEORIES OF FAILURE

formulas.label: VON MISES STRESS (2D)
formulas.text: σ_VM = √(σ₁² − σ₁σ₂ + σ₂²)  |  For bending+torsion: σ_VM = √(σ² + 3τ²)

cards.tip: In design: Use Tresca for safe (conservative) design; von Mises for economical (accurate) design.

cards.heading: THIN CYLINDER (t < d/20)

formulas.label: HOOP / CIRCUMFERENTIAL STRESS
formulas.text: σ_h = pd/(2t)  (acts along circumference)

formulas.label: LONGITUDINAL / AXIAL STRESS
formulas.text: σ_L = pd/(4t)  (acts along axis)

points: σ_h = 2 × σ_L (hoop stress is twice longitudinal)
Cylinder fails along longitudinal seam (hoop stress governs)
Hoop stress resisted by longitudinal joints; Longitudinal stress by circumferential joints

cards.heading: THIN SPHERICAL SHELL

formulas.label: SPHERICAL SHELL STRESSES
formulas.text: σ = pd/(4t)  (same in all directions; no shear in any plane)

formulas.label: CHANGE IN DIAMETER
formulas.text: δd/d = pd(1−μ)/(4tE)

formulas.label: CHANGE IN VOLUME
formulas.text: δV/V = 3pd(1−μ)/(4tE)

points: Sphere is most efficient pressure vessel shape
Hemispherical ends on cylinders: reduces stress at ends from pd/2t to pd/4t
cards.tip: Why hemispherical ends on cylinders? Reduces stress at ends from pd/2t to pd/4t — ends are safe with thinner material.

cards.heading: THICK CYLINDER (LAME'S EQUATIONS)

formulas.label: LAME'S EQUATIONS
formulas.text: σ_radial = a − b/r²  |  σ_hoop = a + b/r²

points: Constants a and b determined from boundary conditions (pressure at inner and outer radii)
Hoop stress varies from MAX at inner radius to MIN at outer radius
Radial stress varies from −p_i at inner to 0 at outer (for internal pressure only)
Compound cylinder: two thick cylinders shrunk fit — reduces max hoop stress at inner surface
Used for gun barrels, high-pressure vessels
cards.alert: For thick cylinders (t > d/20): cannot assume uniform stress. Must use Lame's equations. Hoop stress varies from max at inner to min at outer.

cards.heading: CASTIGLIANO'S THEOREMS

formulas.label: 1st Theorem
formulas.text: ∂U/∂P = δ  (deflection at point of load P in direction of P)

formulas.label: 2nd Theorem
formulas.text: ∂U/∂M = θ  (rotation at point of moment M)

formulas.label: Strain Energy U
formulas.text: U = ∫M²dx/2EI (bending) + ∫P²L/2AE (axial) + ∫T²L/2GJ (torsion)

points: 1st theorem: partial derivative of strain energy w.r.t. a force = deflection at that force's point
2nd theorem (Least Work): for redundant structures, ∂U/∂R = 0 (redundant R makes U minimum)
If no load acts at desired point → apply fictitious load P, find δ, then set P = 0
cards.alert: Castigliano's 2nd theorem (Least Work) is used to find redundants in indeterminate structures — ∂U/∂R = 0

cards.heading: UNIT LOAD METHOD (VIRTUAL WORK)

formulas.label: Deflection by Unit Load
formulas.text: δ = ∫(Mm/EI) dx

formulas.label: Slope by Unit Load
formulas.text: θ = ∫(Mm̄/EI) dx

points: M = bending moment due to actual loading
m = bending moment due to unit load at desired point
m̄ = bending moment due to unit moment at desired point
Most versatile method — works for any structure

cards.heading: PRINCIPLE OF VIRTUAL WORK
points: External virtual work = Internal virtual work
W_ext = W_int → used for deflection calculation
Compatible deformation required (geometry)
Equilibrium of virtual forces required
Applies to both determinate and indeterminate structures

cards.heading: SLOPE DEFLECTION EQUATIONS

formulas.label: General SDE
formulas.text: M_AB = 2EI/L (2θA + θB − 3ψ) + FEM_AB

formulas.label: Where ψ
formulas.text: ψ = Δ/L = sway (relative settlement / length)

points: θA, θB = slopes at ends A and B of member AB
ψ = chord rotation (sway angle) = Δ/L
FEM = Fixed End Moment (from tables)
Two equations per member (near end + far end)
Equilibrium condition: ΣM at each joint = 0
cards.tip: Sign convention: clockwise moments positive. θ and ψ positive clockwise.

cards.heading: FIXED END MOMENTS (FEM) — Standard Cases

rows: Eccentric load P at 'a' from A
UVL (0 to w, A to B)

cards.alert: Fixed end carries more moment than simply supported. FEM signs: near end sagging = positive.

cards.heading: PROCEDURE — SLOPE DEFLECTION METHOD
points: 1. Identify unknown joint rotations (θ) and sway (ψ)
2. Write SDE for each member end (2 equations per member)
3. Apply joint equilibrium: ΣM at each joint = 0
4. For sway frames: apply shear equilibrium (story equation)
5. Solve simultaneous equations for θ and ψ
6. Back-substitute into SDE to find all end moments

cards.heading: KEY TERMS IN MOMENT DISTRIBUTION

rows: Moment to cause unit rotation at near end
Fraction of unbalanced moment distributed to each member

cards.alert: DF of a fixed end = 0 (doesn't rotate). DF of free end = 1 (fully releases). ΣDF at any joint = 1.

cards.heading: MOMENT DISTRIBUTION PROCEDURE
points: 1. Calculate stiffness k for each member at every joint
2. Calculate Distribution Factors DF = k/Σk at each joint
3. Calculate Fixed End Moments (FEMs) for all spans
4. Unlock joints one at a time: distribute unbalanced moment using DF
5. Carry over: half of distributed moment goes to far end (COF = 0.5)
6. Repeat until moments are small (convergence)
7. Sum all FEMs + distributed moments + carry-overs for final moments
cards.tip: Propped cantilever stiffness = 3EI/L. Fixed beam stiffness = 4EI/L. Always use modified stiffness for near-pinned end.

cards.heading: SWAY CORRECTION IN MDM
points: Non-sway: apply MDM directly with DF and COF
Sway frames: MDM in two parts — (1) Non-sway analysis, (2) Sway analysis with unit sway
Final moments = Non-sway moments + k × Sway moments
Check: ΣH = 0 (horizontal equilibrium) to find k

cards.heading: KANI'S METHOD (ROTATION CONTRIBUTION METHOD)

formulas.label: Rotation Factor
formulas.text: r_AB = −k_AB / (2Σk at A)

formulas.label: Rotation Contribution
formulas.text: M'_AB = r_AB × (F_AB + Σ rotation contributions at A from all members)

formulas.label: Final Moment
formulas.text: M_AB = F_AB + 2M'_AB + M'_BA

points: More rapid convergence than MDM for multi-story frames
Particularly suitable for sway frames
Kani's converges faster than MDM — preferred for multi-story multi-bay frames
cards.tip: Kani's gives same result as MDM but converges faster for symmetric frames.

cards.heading: COLUMN ANALOGY METHOD
points: Converts indeterminate structure to an 'analogous column' under eccentric load
The 'column' has: area = ∫ds/EI, section properties from M/EI diagram
Used for fixed arches, closed frames, and complex indeterminate structures
Analogous column stress = redundant force/moment at chosen section

cards.heading: INFLUENCE LINE BASICS

formulas.label: Definition
formulas.text: IL for a function = diagram showing value of that function as unit load moves across span

formulas.label: IL for BM at section
formulas.text: Peak ordinate at section = a × b / L

points: IL for reaction: triangular, peak = 1 at that support
IL for SF at section: triangular with jump of 1 at the section
IL for BM at section: triangular with peak = a×b/L at the section
IL ordinate × actual load = value of function for that load position
For UDL: integrate IL ordinate over loaded length × w

cards.heading: MULLER-BRESLAU'S PRINCIPLE

formulas.label: Principle
formulas.text: IL for any function = deflected shape when the corresponding restraint is removed and unit deformation is given

points: For reaction: remove support, give unit settlement → deflected shape is IL
For SF at section: cut beam at section, give unit relative displacement → IL for SF
For BM at section: insert hinge at section, give unit relative rotation → IL for BM
Extremely powerful for drawing ILs for indeterminate structures

cards.heading: ROLLING LOADS & ABSOLUTE MAX BM

formulas.label: Absolute Max BM (Single load P)
formulas.text: Occurs at midspan = PL/4

formulas.label: Absolute Max BM (Two loads)
formulas.text: Midspan bisects distance between resultant and adjacent critical load

points: Series of concentrated loads: max BM under any load when that load is at its IL peak
Critical position of UDL: load the +ve IL region for max +ve BM
For trusses: IL for member force = IL for SF in corresponding equivalent beam
cards.alert: Position for absolute max BM: resultant R and adjacent load P equidistant from midspan (midspan bisects R–P distance)

cards.heading: THREE-HINGED ARCH (Statically Determinate)

formulas.label: Horizontal Thrust H
formulas.text: H = M_ss / y_c  (M_ss = BM of equivalent SS beam, y_c = rise at crown)

formulas.label: For UDL on parabolic arch
formulas.text: H = wL²/8h  (h = rise at crown)

formulas.label: BM in arch
formulas.text: M_arch = M_ss − H×y

points: Reactions: 4 unknowns (2H + 2V), 4 equations → Statically DETERMINATE
At crown hinge: BM = 0 always (hinge condition)
Parabolic arch under UDL → pure axial compression, zero bending moment everywhere
Temperature rise in two-hinged arch → increases horizontal thrust
cards.alert: In arch, BM = (SS beam BM) − (H × rise at that point). Arch reduces BM significantly → efficient structure.

cards.heading: TWO-HINGED ARCH (1° Indeterminate)

formulas.label: Horizontal Thrust H
formulas.text: H = ∫M_ss × y ds/EI ÷ ∫y² ds/EI

points: One degree indeterminate — H is the redundant
Temperature rise ΔT: additional thrust = EAαΔT / (1 + EI/Ar × ∫y²ds)
Rib shortening reduces thrust compared to rigid arch

cards.heading: FIXED ARCH & ARCH TYPES SUMMARY

rows: H, V, M at each abutment — 6 reactions, 3 equations
Horizontal thrust taken by tie rod

cards.tip: Parabolic arch is the funicular shape for UDL — pure compression, no bending.

cards.heading: STIFFNESS METHOD (DISPLACEMENT METHOD)

formulas.label: Where
formulas.text: [K] = stiffness matrix, {D} = displacement vector, {F} = force vector

points: [K] is always symmetric (from Maxwell's reciprocal theorem)
Diagonal elements k_ii = sum of stiffnesses of members at DOF i
Unknowns are displacements {D} — solve [K]{D} = {F} → {D} = [K]⁻¹{F}
Member end forces from back-substitution
FEM (Finite Element Method) is based on the stiffness method

cards.heading: FLEXIBILITY METHOD (FORCE METHOD)

formulas.label: Flexibility Equation
formulas.text: [f]{X} = {D₀}  (compatibility equations for redundants)

formulas.label: Flexibility coefficient
formulas.text: f_ij = deflection at i due to unit load at j

points: [f] is always symmetric (from Maxwell's reciprocal theorem)
Unknowns are FORCES (redundants {X})
Number of unknowns = degree of static indeterminacy

cards.heading: COMPARISON: STIFFNESS vs FLEXIBILITY
cards.tip: Stiffness and flexibility matrices are INVERSE of each other: [K] = [f]⁻¹. Both are symmetric from Maxwell's theorem.

cards.heading: PLASTIC HINGE & SECTION PROPERTIES

formulas.label: Shape Factor f
formulas.text: f = Mp / My = Zp / Ze

formulas.label: Plastic hinges for collapse
formulas.text: No. of hinges = Degree of Indeterminacy + 1

points: Plastic moment Mp = σy × Zp (plastic section modulus)
Yield moment My = σy × Ze (elastic section modulus)
Plastic hinge forms when entire cross-section reaches yield stress
Plastic section modulus Zp = A/2 × (ȳt + ȳb)
cards.alert: Shape factor for rectangle = 1.5. For I-section ≈ 1.15. Rectangle is most asked!

cards.heading: LOAD FACTOR & THEOREMS OF PLASTIC ANALYSIS

formulas.label: Load Factor λ
formulas.text: λ = Collapse load Wc / Working load W

points: Lower Bound Theorem: Equilibrium + yield condition satisfied → ≤ true collapse load (safe estimate)
Upper Bound Theorem: Any collapse mechanism with work balance → ≥ true collapse load (unsafe estimate)
Uniqueness Theorem: Collapse load satisfies equilibrium + yield + mechanism conditions
True collapse load lies between lower and upper bounds

cards.heading: COLLAPSE MECHANISMS — STANDARD CASES

rows: SS beam, eccentric load (a from A, b from B)

cards.tip: Fixed beam collapse load = 2 × SS beam collapse load. Remember: 4Mp/L (SS) vs 8Mp/L (fixed) for central load.

formulas.label: YOUNG'S MODULUS
formulas.text: E = 2 × 10⁵ MPa (200 GPa) — same for ALL structural steels

formulas.label: SHEAR MODULUS
formulas.text: G = 0.77 × 10⁵ MPa ≈ 77 GPa

formulas.label: DENSITY
formulas.text: 7850 kg/m³ (78.5 kN/m³)

cards.heading: IS 800:2007 — LIMIT STATE DESIGN BASICS

formulas.label: PARTIAL SAFETY FACTORS
formulas.text: γ_m0 = 1.10 (yielding) | γ_m1 = 1.25 (ultimate/fracture) | γ_mw = 1.25 (shop weld) / 1.50 (site weld) | γ_mb = 1.25 (bolts)

formulas.label: LOAD FACTORS
formulas.text: DL+LL: 1.5 each | DL+WL: 1.5 each or 0.9DL+1.5WL (uplift)

points: Section classification: Class 1 (Plastic) → Class 2 (Compact) → Class 3 (Semi-compact) → Class 4 (Slender)
Only Class 1 sections can be used for plastic analysis (adequate rotation capacity)
Limit State of Strength: yielding, buckling, fracture (collapse)
Limit State of Serviceability: deflection, vibration, durability
cards.tip: Slenderness ratio λ ≤ 180 for axially loaded compression members (IS 800).

cards.heading: BOLT TYPES & PROPERTIES

formulas.label: BOLT GRADE DECODE
formulas.text: Grade X.Y → f_ub = X × 100 MPa, f_yb = X × Y × 10 MPa

formulas.label: SHEAR CAPACITY (single shear, threads)
formulas.text: V_dsb = (f_ub/√3) × A_nb / γ_mb  where A_nb ≈ 0.78 × A_shank

formulas.label: BEARING CAPACITY
formulas.text: V_dpb = 2.5 × k_b × d × t × f_u / γ_mb  (f_u of PLATE, not bolt)

cards.alert: HSFG bolts: load transferred by FRICTION at interface (no slip). Used for vibration-prone, fatigue-critical connections.

cards.heading: BOLT SPACING & DETAILING

formulas.label: COMBINED SHEAR + TENSION
formulas.text: (V/V_db)² + (T/T_db)² ≤ 1.0

formulas.label: TENSION CAPACITY
formulas.text: T_db = 0.90 × f_ub × A_n / γ_mb

cards.tip: d_o = nominal hole diameter = d + clearance. ε = √(250/f_y).

cards.heading: TYPES OF WELDS & FILLET WELD DESIGN

formulas.label: THROAT THICKNESS
formulas.text: Throat = 0.7 × s  (s = fillet weld size, for equal-leg)

formulas.label: EFFECTIVE LENGTH
formulas.text: L_eff = L − 2s  (deduct 2 × size for craters)

formulas.label: DESIGN STRENGTH per unit length
formulas.text: P_dw = 0.7s × f_u / (√3 × γ_mw)  [shear on throat plane]

cards.alert: ⚠ Fillet welds CANNOT be used where members meet at angles < 60° or > 120°.

cards.heading: BUTT WELD & √3 FACTOR

formulas.label: FULL-PENETRATION BUTT WELD (tension)
formulas.text: Strength = throat × L × f_y / γ_m0 (= parent plate capacity)

formulas.label: WHY √3 IN FILLET WELD FORMULA
formulas.text: Fillet fails by shear on throat → von Mises: τ_y = f_u/√3 converts UTS to shear strength

points: Full-penetration butt weld: develops full strength of parent plate
Partial-penetration butt weld: reduced effective throat
Site welds have higher γ_mw (1.50 vs 1.25) — less reliable than shop welds

cards.heading: THREE FAILURE MODES (IS 800:2007)

formulas.label: DESIGN STRENGTH
formulas.text: T_d = MINIMUM of T_dg, T_dn, T_db

points: Net area: A_n = A_g − Σ(d_o × t). For staggered holes: add Σ(p_s²·t/4g) (Cochrane's)
Shear lag factor α: 1 bolt = 0.6, 2 bolts = 0.7, 3+ bolts = 0.8
Block shear: a 'block' pulled out simultaneously in tension + shear
cards.alert: Always check ALL THREE failure modes. Block shear often governs for short connections with closely spaced bolts.

cards.heading: EULER & DESIGN BUCKLING

formulas.label: EULER'S CRITICAL LOAD
formulas.text: P_E = π²EI / L_e²  |  σ_E = π²E / λ²  (λ = L_e/r_min)

formulas.label: DESIGN COMPRESSIVE STRENGTH
formulas.text: P_d = χ × A_g × f_y / γ_m0

points: IS 800 uses 4 buckling curves: a (α=0.21), b (0.34), c (0.49), d (0.76)
Circular hollow section → curve 'a' (best, lowest imperfection)
Welded I-section about minor axis → curve 'd' (worst)

cards.heading: BUILT-UP COLUMNS

formulas.label: EFFECTIVE SLENDERNESS
formulas.text: λ_e = √(λ_o² + λ_c²)  (λ_o = overall, λ_c = single component)

formulas.label: TRANSVERSE SHEAR
formulas.text: V_b = 2.5% of column axial load (for lacing/batten design)

points: Lacing/battens connect components and transfer transverse shear
Column buckles about axis of MINIMUM moment of inertia
CHS (circular hollow) is most efficient — same r about all axes
cards.tip: Built-up columns increase radius of gyration about weak axis. Connections must transfer shear (lacing/battens carry transverse shear).

cards.heading: LATERALLY SUPPORTED BEAMS — DESIGN STRENGTH

formulas.label: DESIGN BENDING STRENGTH (M_d)
formulas.text: Class 1 & 2: M_d = β_b × Z_p × f_y / γ_m0 (β_b = 1.0)

formulas.label: Class 3 (Semi-compact)
formulas.text: M_d = Z_e × f_y / γ_m0

formulas.label: DESIGN SHEAR STRENGTH
formulas.text: V_d = A_v × f_yw / (√3 × γ_m0)  (von Mises shear yield)

points: Laterally supported = compression flange restrained from lateral movement (e.g., embedded in slab)
Shape factor for I-section ≈ 1.12–1.14
High shear (V > 0.6V_d) reduces moment capacity

cards.heading: LATERAL TORSIONAL BUCKLING (LTB)

formulas.label: M_cr
formulas.text: M_cr = (π²EI_y/L²)√[I_w/I_y + L²GI_t/(π²EI_y)]

points: LTB occurs in laterally UNSUPPORTED beams — compression flange displaces sideways + twists
If λ_LT ≤ 0.4 → χ_LT = 1.0 → no LTB reduction (full M_p available)
Stocky web (d/t_w ≤ 67ε): no shear buckling check needed
Intermediate stiffeners needed when d/t_w > 200
cards.alert: Stiffeners required: bearing stiffeners under heavy loads, intermediate stiffeners if d/t_w > 200.

cards.heading: PLATE GIRDER OVERVIEW
points: Deep built-up I-section used when rolled sections are inadequate (spans > 12–15 m, heavy loads)
Web carries ~90% shear; Flanges carry ~90% bending moment
Typical d/L ratio: buildings 1/10 to 1/14; bridges 1/8 to 1/12
Web slenderness d/t_w = 200–300 (vs 30–60 for rolled sections)

formulas.label: ECONOMICAL DEPTH
formulas.text: d_opt ≈ k × (M/f_y·t_w)^(1/3)  where k ≈ 1.0–1.2

cards.heading: STIFFENERS & TENSION FIELD
points: Bearing stiffeners: at supports and under concentrated loads (designed as columns, L_e = 0.7d)
Intermediate transverse stiffeners: prevent web shear buckling (spacing 0.74d to 1.5d)
Tension field action: after elastic buckling, web develops diagonal tension bands (like Pratt truss)
End panel needs bearing stiffener always (anchors the tension field)
Flange splices placed at low-moment regions (not midspan)

cards.heading: TYPES OF COLUMN BASES

rows: Light/moderate axial loads, no significant moment
Base plate + gusset plates + cleat angles
Very heavy loads on weak soil
Two-tier perpendicular steel beams encased in concrete

formulas.label: BEARING PRESSURE ON CONCRETE
formulas.text: σ_bearing = 0.6 × f_ck  (for M20: 0.6 × 20 = 12 MPa)

formulas.label: REQUIRED BASE PLATE AREA
formulas.text: A = P / σ_bearing

formulas.label: PLATE THICKNESS
formulas.text: t_s = √(2.5w(a² − 0.3b²)γ_m0/f_y)  (w = pressure, a/b = projections)

points: Minimum 4 anchor bolts (one near each corner)
Anchor bolt embedment: 12–20 × bolt diameter
Eccentricity e > L/6: some bolts go into tension
For pure compression: bolts resist erection forces and wind uplift only
cards.tip: For pure axial load: bolts only resist erection forces. For uplift/moment: bolts are critical structural elements.

cards.heading: ROOF TRUSS TYPES

rows: Curved top chord (parabolic), very efficient for UDL

points: Typical truss spacing: 3–6 m
Pitch (rise/span): 1/4 to 1/8 (steeper for snow regions)
Self-weight estimate ≈ Span/3 kg/m² of plan area
Roof live load (IS 875 Part 2): 0.75 kN/m² for slope ≤ 10°

cards.heading: BRACING & WIND LOADS

formulas.label: CRITICAL UPLIFT COMBINATION
formulas.text: 0.9DL + 1.5WL  (minimizes DL while wind fully factored)

points: Plan bracing at top chord level → transfers wind loads from gable to braced bays
X-bracing in side walls → resists lateral forces (wind, seismic)
Sag rods: reduce deflection of purlins along weak axis
Gantry girders: designed for vertical wheel loads + lateral surge (10% LL) + longitudinal force (5% wheel load)
cards.tip: Bracing prevents the truss frame from racking under wind/earthquake.

cards.heading: PLASTIC THEORY FUNDAMENTALS

formulas.label: YIELD MOMENT
formulas.text: M_y = Z_e × f_y (extreme fibre yields)

formulas.label: PLASTIC MOMENT
formulas.text: M_p = Z_p × f_y (entire section at f_y)

formulas.label: SHAPE FACTOR
formulas.text: s = M_p/M_y = Z_p/Z_e

points: Plastic neutral axis divides section into TWO EQUAL AREAS
Hinges for collapse = DI + 1
Class 1 sections required for plastic analysis (adequate rotation capacity)

cards.heading: COLLAPSE LOADS & THEOREMS
points: Lower bound (static): equilibrium + M ≤ Mp → SAFE estimate
Upper bound (kinematic): mechanism → UNSAFE estimate
True collapse = minimum from kinematic approach = maximum from static approach
LSD is more economical than WSM: uses M_p instead of M_y (50% more for rectangle)

topics.desc: Permissible stresses · Modular ratio · Neutral axis · Balanced/Under/Over-reinforced

topics.name: Limit State Method (LSM)
topics.desc: IS 456:2000 · Partial safety factors · Stress block · Xu,max

topics.desc: Effective depth · Ast · Doubly reinforced · Flange width of T-beam

topics.desc: Nominal shear stress · Stirrups · Development length Ld · Torsion

topics.desc: One-way · Two-way · Flat slab · l/d ratio · IS 456 Cl.23

topics.desc: Short/Long column · Effective length · Ties · Helical · Isolated footing

topics.desc: Mix design · w/c ratio · Workability tests · Cover · Curing · SCC · FRC

cards.heading: WSM FUNDAMENTALS

formulas.label: CRITICAL NA DEPTH
formulas.text: x_c/d = m·σ_cbc / (m·σ_cbc + σ_st)  |  M20+Fe415: x_c/d = 0.289

formulas.label: ACTUAL NA DEPTH
formulas.text: b·x_a²/2 = m·Ast·(d − x_a)  (equate moments of area about NA)

points: Assumptions: Plane sections remain plane; concrete takes NO tension; linear stress-strain; perfect bond
Balanced: x_a = x_c (steel & concrete reach limits simultaneously)
Under-reinforced: x_a < x_c (steel governs — DUCTILE, preferred)
Over-reinforced: x_a > x_c (concrete governs — BRITTLE, avoided)

cards.heading: IS 456 PERMISSIBLE VALUES (WSM)

formulas.label: MOMENT OF RESISTANCE (Balanced)
formulas.text: M_r = b·x_c·(σ_cbc/2)·(d − x_c/3) = Ast·σ_st·(d − x_c/3)

formulas.label: LEVER ARM FACTOR
formulas.text: j = 1 − k/3  where k = x_c/d

points: Fe250 (MS): σ_st = 140 N/mm² (tension), 130 N/mm² (compression)
Fe415 (HYSD): σ_st = 230 N/mm² (tension), 190 N/mm² (compression)
cards.alert: ⚠ Actual neutral axis found by: b·x_a²/2 = m·Ast·(d − x_a). Compare with x_c to classify as under/over/balanced.

cards.heading: LSM KEY VALUES (Memorise)

formulas.label: PARTIAL SAFETY FACTORS
formulas.text: γ_c (concrete) = 1.5  |  γ_s (steel) = 1.15

formulas.label: DESIGN STRESS
formulas.text: Concrete: 0.446f_ck  |  Steel: 0.87f_y

points: Factored loads: DL×1.5 + LL×1.5 (most common); 0.9DL + 1.5WL (uplift)
Stress block: Parabolic (0 to 3ε_c/7) + Rectangular (3ε_c/7 to ε_cu); max strain ε_cu = 0.0035
Strain in steel at failure: ε_s ≥ 0.002 + 0.87f_y/E_s (ensures ductile failure)

cards.heading: Mu,lim FORMULA & TENSION STEEL

formulas.label: LIMITING MOMENT OF RESISTANCE
formulas.text: Mu,lim = 0.36·f_ck·b·Xu,max·(d − 0.416·Xu,max)

formulas.label: FOR M20+Fe415
formulas.text: Mu,lim = 2.76 bd²  (N/mm units)

formulas.label: Ast EQUATION
formulas.text: 0.87·f_y·Ast·d·(1 − Ast·f_y/(b·d·f_ck)) = Mu

cards.alert: ⚠ Max Ast in beam = 0.04bD (IS 456). Max Asc in column = 0.06Ag.

cards.heading: SINGLY & DOUBLY REINFORCED BEAMS

formulas.label: T-BEAM FLANGE WIDTH (IS 456)
formulas.text: Interior: b_f = l₀/6 + b_w + 6D_f  |  Edge (L-beam): b_f = l₀/12 + b_w + 3D_f

formulas.label: DOUBLY REINFORCED — EXTRA MOMENT
formulas.text: M₂ = Mu − Mu,lim  (carried by compression steel)

points: Doubly reinforced: used when depth is restricted AND M > Mu,lim, or moment reversal (earthquake)
Compression steel reduces long-term creep deflection significantly
If Xu < D_f (NA within flange): T-beam behaves as rectangular beam of width b_f
Effective cover: clear cover + ½ bar dia; typically 25–50 mm
Minimum tension steel: 0.85bd/f_y (prevents sudden brittle failure)

cards.heading: DETAILING RULES
points: Maximum tension/compression steel in beam: 4% of bD (IS 456 Cl.26.5.1.1)
Bar must extend beyond theoretical cut-off point by: 12φ or d (whichever greater)
In continuous beams: ≥ 1/3 of midspan positive steel must extend into support
Side face reinforcement for beams depth > 750mm: 0.1% of web area on each face
Effective span of SS beam: min of (clear span + d) and (c/c of supports)

cards.heading: SHEAR IN RCC BEAMS

formulas.label: NOMINAL SHEAR STRESS
formulas.text: τ_v = Vu/(b·d)

formulas.label: STIRRUP SPACING
formulas.text: Sv = 0.87·f_y·Asv·d / Vus  |  Max spacing: 0.75d or 300mm (whichever less)

formulas.label: SHEAR RESISTED BY STIRRUPS
formulas.text: Vus = Vu − τ_c·b·d  (concrete takes τ_c·b·d)

cards.alert: ⚠ τ_v shall NOT exceed τ_c,max — else redesign section. τ_c depends on BOTH concrete grade AND % tension steel.

cards.heading: DEVELOPMENT LENGTH & BOND

formulas.label: DEVELOPMENT LENGTH (IS 456)
formulas.text: Ld = φ·σ_st/(4·τ_bd)  |  For Fe415 in M20 (LSM): Ld ≈ 47φ

formulas.label: DEFORMED BAR BOND INCREASE
formulas.text: Tension: 60% increase over plain bars  |  Compression: 25% increase

points: Hook allowance = 16φ (standard 180° hook) — mandatory for plain bars
Internal bend radius: Fe250 = 2φ; Fe415/Fe500 = 4φ

cards.heading: TORSION (IS 456 Cl.41)

formulas.label: EQUIVALENT BM
formulas.text: Me1 = Mu + Mt  where  Mt = Tu(1 + D/b)/1.7

formulas.label: Me2
formulas.text: Me2 = Mt − Mu  (if positive, add compression steel)

points: Compatibility torsion: arises from deformation compatibility with adjacent members (can be reduced by 25%)
Equilibrium torsion: must be fully designed for (stability depends on it)
Cranked (bent-up) bars at 45°: resist both shear and negative moment at supports

cards.heading: SLAB TYPES & CLASSIFICATION

formulas.label: ONE-WAY vs TWO-WAY
formulas.text: ly/lx ≤ 2 → Two-way slab  |  ly/lx > 2 → One-way slab

formulas.label: EFFECTIVE SPAN
formulas.text: Min of: (clear span + d) and (c/c of supports)

points: Distribution steel in one-way slab: 0.12% of bD (HYSD), 0.15% (MS)
Flat slab: no beams, column head with drop panel
Drop panel: each side ≥ L/3 of panel span; depth below slab ≥ D/4
Column head diameter in flat slab ≥ 0.25 × smaller span

cards.heading: DEFLECTION CONTROL — l/d RATIO
points: For spans > 10 m: multiply basic l/d by 10/span
Modification factors apply for steel stress and compression steel
Deflection limit: span/250 total; span/350 or 20mm after construction
Shear reinforcement in slabs usually NOT required (large width → low τ_v)
cards.alert: ⚠ l/d check is for span/250 deflection limit. Actual deflection: span/350 or 20 mm max.

cards.heading: FLAT SLAB & PUNCHING SHEAR

formulas.label: PUNCHING SHEAR CRITICAL SECTION
formulas.text: Perimeter at d/2 from column face

formulas.label: PERMISSIBLE PUNCHING STRESS
formulas.text: τ_p = 0.25√f_ck  (N/mm²)

points: One-way shear critical section: at d from face of support
Two-way (punching) shear: at d/2 from column face
Minimum 3 panels in each direction required for flat slab system
Two-way slab: shorter span carries more load (stiffer); yield line at 45° from corners

formulas.label: SHORT vs LONG
formulas.text: Short: leff/D ≤ 12 AND leff/b ≤ 12  |  Long: leff/D > 12 (additional moment)

formulas.label: LOAD CAPACITY (IS 456 Cl.39.3)
formulas.text: Pu = 0.4·f_ck·Ac + 0.67·f_y·Asc  (tied column)

formulas.label: HELICAL COLUMN
formulas.text: Pu = 1.05 × (tied column capacity)

formulas.label: MIN ECCENTRICITY
formulas.text: e_min = L/500 + D/30 or 20 mm (whichever greater)

cards.heading: FOOTINGS — PUNCHING SHEAR & COVER

formulas.label: CRITICAL FOR BM
formulas.text: At face of column

formulas.label: CRITICAL FOR ONE-WAY SHEAR
formulas.text: At d from face of column

formulas.label: CRITICAL FOR PUNCHING SHEAR
formulas.text: Perimeter at d/2 from column face  |  τ_p = 0.25√f_ck

points: Minimum cover in footings: 50 mm ALWAYS (IS 456 Cl.26.4.2.2)
Braced column: lateral loads resisted by shear walls, not column itself
Volume of helix reinforcement ≥ 0.36(Ag/Ac − 1)(f_ck/f_y)

cards.heading: PRESTRESS SYSTEMS & LOSSES

formulas.label: PRE-TENSIONING
formulas.text: Tendons stressed BEFORE concreting (factory; bond transfer)

formulas.label: POST-TENSIONING
formulas.text: Tendons stressed AFTER concreting (in-situ; anchorage transfer)

formulas.label: STRESS AT FIBRE
formulas.text: σ = P/A ± Pe·y/I ± M·y/I

points: Friction loss and anchorage slip: occur ONLY in post-tensioned members
Elastic shortening: higher in pre-tensioned (all tendons lose simultaneously)
For single-tendon post-tensioned: no elastic shortening loss
Total prestress losses ≈ 15–20% of initial prestress
Kern (core): region where load must fall to avoid tension anywhere → rectangle: ±d/6 from centroid

cards.heading: LOAD BALANCING & MINIMUM GRADES

formulas.label: LOAD BALANCING
formulas.text: Parabolic cable generates upward w_e = 8Pe/L²  (balances self-weight)

formulas.label: MODULUS OF RUPTURE
formulas.text: f_cr = 0.7√f_ck  (IS 456)

points: Minimum concrete grade: Pre-tensioned = M40; Post-tensioned = M30
Prestressing wire UTS: 1500–2000 N/mm² (vs Fe415 ≈ 485 N/mm²)
Relaxation: time-dependent decrease in stress at constant strain (steel property)
Freyssinet system (conical wedge anchorage): widely used in India for post-tensioning

cards.heading: WORKABILITY TESTS

formulas.label: TARGET MEAN STRENGTH
formulas.text: f_target = f_ck + 1.65s  (IS 10262; s = standard deviation)

rows: Most common; not for very dry/very wet

points: Abram's w/c ratio law: Higher w/c → lower strength
SCC (Self Compacting Concrete): high fluidity, no segregation; uses superplasticizer + VMA
FRC (Fibre Reinforced Concrete): improves toughness and crack resistance (steel/polypropylene fibres)

cards.heading: COVER, CURING & DURABILITY (IS 456)
points: Footings cover: ALWAYS 50 mm minimum
Curing period: OPC min 7 days; blended (slag/pozzolanic) min 14 days
Max chloride content: RCC = 0.4% by mass of cement; PSC = 0.1%
Sampling: 1 sample/5m³ or per floor; each sample = 3 cubes at 28 days

cards.heading: OPC COMPOUNDS (Bogue's)

formulas.label: BOGUE COMPOUNDS (Decreasing %)
formulas.text:
C₃S (Alite) ≈ 45% — Early strength, high heat
C₂S (Belite) ≈ 25% — Late strength, low heat
C₃A (Aluminate) ≈ 11% — Very fast, causes sulphate attack
C₄AF (Ferrite) ≈ 8% — Least heat; colour of cement
Gypsum ≈ 3–4% — Controls setting time (retards C₃A)

points: Strength order: C₃S > C₂S > C₃A > C₄AF (for 28-day strength)
Heat order: C₃A > C₃S > C₄AF > C₂S
Slow setting: Higher C₂S; High early: Higher C₃S; Low heat: Low C₃A + C₃S

cards.heading: OPC GRADES & TESTS

formulas.label: OPC GRADES (IS 269, IS 8112, IS 12269)
formulas.text:
33 Grade: Min 28-day strength = 33 N/mm²
43 Grade: Min 28-day strength = 43 N/mm²
53 Grade: Min 28-day strength = 53 N/mm²
Standard weight of cement bag = 50 kg

formulas.label: VICAT APPARATUS TESTS
formulas.text:
Normal consistency: Plunger 10 mm from bottom
Initial setting time: Penetration 5–7 mm from bottom; Min = 30 min
Final setting time: Annular needle no impression; Max = 600 min (10 hr)

cards.alert: ⚠ Soundness test (Le Chatelier / Autoclave) checks for free CaO and MgO — causes expansion and cracking after setting. Max expansion = 10 mm (Le Chatelier).

formulas.label: TYPES & KEY FEATURE
formulas.text:
Rapid Hardening Cement: Higher C₃S, finer grinding; 3-day strength = 28-day OPC
Low Heat Cement: Low C₃A + C₃S; used in mass concrete/dams
Sulphate Resistant Cement: Very low C₃A (<5%); for marine/aggressive soil
White Cement: Low C₄AF; uses pure limestone + china clay
Quick Setting Cement: Al₂SO₄ added; sets in 5 min; used underwater

cards.heading: AGGREGATE TESTS (IS 2386)

formulas.label: KEY AGGREGATE TESTS
formulas.text:
Crushing value: %wear under 400kN load; Roads ≤30%, Concrete ≤45%
Impact value: Resistance to sudden load (hammer); Roads ≤30%
Abrasion (Los Angeles): Resistance to wear; Roads ≤30–40%
Specific gravity of aggregates: 2.6–2.7 (most stones)

formulas.label: STONE TESTS
formulas.text:
Acid test: 1% HCl for 7 days — calcareous stones effervesce
Smith's test: Water turns brown — clay/soluble material present
Crystallisation: Na₂SO₄ solution (5 cycles) — durability test

points: Igneous rocks: Granite, Basalt, Trap — strongest, fire resistant
Sedimentary rocks: Limestone, Sandstone, Shale — stratified; Slate for roofing
Metamorphic: Marble (from limestone), Quartzite (from sandstone), Slate (from shale) — hard and durable
For fire-resistant masonry: Granite preferred; limestone avoided (CaCO₃ decomposes at 900°C)
Water absorption of good building stone: should NOT exceed 5%
Flakiness index: % particles with least dimension < 0.6 × mean sieve size; max 25% for bituminous
Well-graded aggregate minimises voids → reduces cement paste needed

cards.heading: BRICKS — TESTS & STANDARDS

formulas.label: BRICK CLASSIFICATION (IS 1077)
formulas.text:
Class I (1st class): Compressive strength ≥ 10.5 N/mm²; absorption ≤ 20%
Class II (2nd class): ≥ 7.0 N/mm²; absorption ≤ 22%
Class III (3rd class): ≥ 3.5 N/mm²; used for non-structural
AAC Blocks: Autoclaved Aerated Concrete; lightweight; good insulation

formulas.label: BRICK TESTS (IS 3495)
formulas.text:
Compressive strength: Wet brick tested; 1st class ≥ 10.5 N/mm²
Water absorption: Immerse 24 hr; ≤ 20% (1st class), ≤ 22% (2nd)
Efflorescence: Caused by soluble sulphates (Na, K, Mg, Ca); Rating: Nil/Slight/Moderate/Heavy/Serious

points: Unit weight of burnt clay bricks: 18–20 kN/m³
Standard metric brick size: 190 × 90 × 90 mm (with 10 mm mortar joint = 200 × 100 × 100 mm nominal)
Acid test of bricks: detects reactive lime nodules (CaCO₃ inclusions) which cause 'lime blowing'

cards.heading: MORTAR DESIGNATIONS (IS 2250) & MASONRY BONDS

formulas.label: MORTAR TYPES — INCREASING STRENGTH
formulas.text:
H3 < H2 < H1 < M3 < M2 < M1 < L3 < L2 < L1
H = Hydraulic lime; M = Mixed/Gauged; L = Cement mortar
Gauging = adding cement to lime mortar to improve quality

formulas.label: MASONRY BONDS
formulas.text:
English bond: Alternating courses of headers + stretchers — STRONGEST
Flemish bond: Alternating header-stretcher in same course — better appearance
Stretcher bond: All stretchers — for ½ brick partitions only
Min wall for double Flemish bond = 1.5 brick (340 mm)

points: Queen closer: placed next to quoin header in English bond to maintain staggered joints
Pozzolana reacts with Ca(OH)₂ released during cement hydration → forms C-S-H gel
Hollow blocks: reduce self-weight 20–40%, good thermal insulation, for non-load-bearing walls
Mortar volume in typical brick wall ≈ 20–25% of total wall volume

cards.heading: TIMBER CLASSIFICATION & PROPERTIES

formulas.label: TREE TYPES
formulas.text:
Exogenous: Growth by outer rings; has heartwood + sapwood + cambium; most structural timber
→ Conifers (Deodar, Chir) — softwood; Deciduous (Teak, Sal) — hardwood
Endogenous: Growth inward (bamboo, cane, palm) — no rings

formulas.label: TIMBER STRENGTH
formulas.text:
Strength is maximum along grain direction
Strength is minimum perpendicular to grain
IS 399: timber weight specified at 12% moisture content

points: Seasoning methods: Natural (air drying — slow); Artificial (kiln — fast, controlled); Water (immersion — removes sap)
Defects: Knot (branch inclusion), Shakes (separation along grain), Warping (cupping, bowing, twisting)
Heart shake: radial crack from centre outward; Cup/Wind shake: separation of annual rings due to wind
Preservation: Creosote oil (pressure impregnation/Bethell process), ASCU, Coal tar
Boucherie process: uses hydraulic pressure to replace sap in green timber with preservative (CuSO₄)
Heartwood: darker, harder, more durable (dead cells with extractives); Sapwood: lighter, less durable
Teak (Tectona grandis): deciduous hardwood, self-preserving (high oil), best Indian structural timber
Plywood: odd number of veneers bonded with grain at 90° (cross-banding) for balanced stress
Moisture content (%) = (Wet weight − Oven dry weight) / Oven dry weight × 100
Fibre Saturation Point (FSP) ≈ 25–30% MC — below FSP, shrinkage occurs as MC decreases

cards.heading: IRON & STEEL

formulas.label: TYPES OF IRON (by Carbon %)
formulas.text:
Pig iron: 3–4% C — brittle, not used structurally; base product
Cast iron: 2–4% C — strong in compression, brittle in tension
Wrought iron: <0.15% C — malleable, tough, weldable, rust resistant
Mild steel: 0.15–0.3% C — most common structural steel

points: Galvanising: coating steel with ZINC (hot-dip at 450°C) for corrosion protection
Zinc provides cathodic (sacrificial) protection even if scratched

cards.heading: PAINT CONSTITUENTS & VARNISH

formulas.label: PAINT CONSTITUENTS
formulas.text:
Base: White lead, red lead, zinc oxide — forms film
Vehicle (Carrier): Linseed oil, tung oil — binds base
Drier: Lead, manganese, cobalt compounds — accelerates drying
Solvent/Thinner: Turpentine — reduces viscosity
Pigment: Colour addition

points: Drier is lead acetate / cobalt naphthenate — NOT lime or calcium carbonate
Distemper: water-based paint, for interior walls ONLY (not weather-resistant)
Varnish: transparent, contains resin + oil/spirit, NO opaque pigment — shows natural grain
Shellac varnish: uses methylated spirit (alcohol) as solvent; dries by evaporation
Copal varnish: uses linseed oil as solvent — 'oil varnish'
Fire-resistant paint: contains antimony oxide / chlorinated rubber (intumescent — swells under heat)

cards.heading: IS 875 LOADS & GLASS TYPES

formulas.label: IS 875 PARTS
formulas.text:
Part 1: Dead loads | Part 2: Imposed (Live) loads
Part 3: Wind loads | Part 4: Snow loads | Part 5: Special loads
Specific weight of concrete: 24 kN/m³ (RCC: 25 kN/m³)

formulas.label: GLASS TYPES
formulas.text:
Crown glass: Common window glass (soda-lime)
Plate glass: Ground and polished — better clarity
Wired glass: Fire resistant — wire mesh embedded
Tempered glass: Heat treated — 4–5× stronger; breaks into granules (safe)
Borosilicate (Pyrex): Contains B₂O₃, high temperature resistance

points: For air-conditioned buildings: double-glazed or reflective (tinted/heat-absorbing) glass recommended
Low-E glass: silver coating reflects infrared heat; reduces cooling load

cards.heading: DAMP PROOF COURSE (DPC)

formulas.label: DPC MATERIALS & APPLICATION
formulas.text:
Flexible: Bitumen felt (most common), lead sheets, copper sheets
Semi-rigid: Mastic asphalt (applied at min 160°C)
Rigid: Dense concrete, slate (natural DPC stone)
Position: Usually at plinth level, around door/window openings

points: Weep holes: in retaining/breast walls — to drain accumulated water from behind wall (75×75 mm or 100 mm dia pipes @ 1.5–3 m spacing)
Cavity wall: double wall with air gap for heat + sound insulation + moisture resistance
Efflorescence: white crystalline deposits on brick/stone — due to soluble sulphates (Na₂SO₄, K₂SO₄, MgSO₄)
Hot bitumen for DPC must be applied at minimum 160°C for proper flow

cards.heading: STAIRS — TERMINOLOGY & RULES

formulas.label: STAIR PROPORTIONING RULES
formulas.text:
2R + T = 60–65 cm (Blondel's formula)
R + T = 40–45 cm
R × T = 400–450 cm²
Riser (R): 15–18 cm; Tread (T): 25–30 cm
Max steps per flight: 12–16 (NBC: not more than 16)

formulas.label: STAIR PARTS
formulas.text:
Riser: Vertical face of step
Tread (Going): Horizontal surface of step
Nosing: Front edge of tread (projecting beyond riser)
Scotia: Moulding below nosing (for aesthetics + grip)
Baluster: Vertical member supporting handrail
Newel post: Large post at start/end of handrail
Soffit: Underside of staircase slab
Waist: Minimum thickness of stair slab

points: NBC maximum ramp slope = 1 in 12 (8.33%) for wheelchair access
Minimum staircase width for residential building = 1.0 m

cards.heading: FOUNDATION & PLINTH

formulas.label: KEY DEPTHS
formulas.text:
Minimum foundation depth for residential buildings: 0.90 m below ground
Minimum plinth height: 0.45 m from centre of road
Rankine's formula: d_min = (q/γ) × [(1−sinφ)/(1+sinφ)]²

points: Footing: lowest structural element that transmits building loads to underlying soil
Types: Isolated (individual column), Combined, Strip/Continuous (wall), Raft/Mat, Pile (deep)
Lintel: horizontal structural member spanning over openings to support masonry above
Minimum lintel bearing on each side = 150 mm (spans < 1.5 m) to 200 mm (spans > 1.5 m)
Eaves: lower edges of sloping roof that overhang beyond the wall face (protect from rain)
Cleats: angular brackets on rafters to prevent purlins from sliding down the slope
Louvred door: slanted slats allowing air circulation while maintaining privacy

cards.heading: EARTHWORK — KEY TERMS
points: Cutting (Excavation): removal of soil/rock above formation level
Filling (Embankment): compacted layers of soil below formation level
Free haul: distance within which earth can be moved at normal rate (usually 30 m)
Lead: horizontal distance of earth transportation
Lift: vertical height through which material is raised
Shrinkage factor: embankment volume > borrow pit volume (loose soil compacts)
Swell factor: cut volume > undisturbed volume (rock swells after blasting)

cards.heading: PRISMOIDAL FORMULA — EARTHWORK VOLUMES

formulas.label: TRAPEZOIDAL (Mean Area) METHOD
formulas.text:
V = L/2 × (A₁ + A₂)
A = (b + sh)h  where b=base, s=side slope, h=depth

formulas.label: PRISMOIDAL FORMULA (more accurate)
formulas.text:
V = L/6 × (A₁ + 4Am + A₂)
Am = mid-section area

formulas.label: PRISMOIDAL CORRECTION
formulas.text:
Cp = L/12 × (b₁−b₂)(h₁−h₂) × s
(subtract for cutting, add for filling)

cards.alert: Prismoidal formula is always more accurate than trapezoidal. UPPSC frequently asks which gives larger volume — trapezoidal gives higher volume for cutting.

cards.heading: COMPACTION — METHODS & EQUIPMENT
points: OMC (Optimum Moisture Content): moisture at which max dry density achieved
MDD (Max Dry Density): determined by Proctor test (IS 2720 Part 7 & 8)
Standard Proctor: 2.5 kg rammer, 305 mm drop, 3 layers, 25 blows
Modified Proctor: 4.9 kg rammer, 450 mm drop, 5 layers, 25 blows
Field compaction control: Sand replacement method (IS 2720 Part 28)

cards.heading: DEWATERING & SHORING
points: Open sump pumping: for small excavations with low water table
Well point system: for sandy soils, lowers water table by vacuum
Deep well dewatering: large excavations, high permeability soils

cards.heading: S-CURVE (PROGRESS CURVE)

formulas.label: CUMULATIVE COST / PROGRESS
formulas.text:
Plotted as: % cumulative work done vs. time
S-shape: slow start → rapid middle → slow finish
Used for: cash flow forecasting, earned value analysis

points: Banana curve: two S-curves (early start + late start) — acceptable zone between them
Earned Value: BCWP (Budgeted Cost of Work Performed) vs ACWP (Actual Cost of Work Performed)

Schedule Variance (SV) = BCWP − BCWS  (+ve = ahead of schedule)

SPI (Schedule Performance Index) = BCWP/BCWS  (>1 = ahead)

cards.heading: CPM — NETWORK ANALYSIS

formulas.label: EARLIEST EVENT TIME (E)
formulas.text:
Forward pass: E_j = max(E_i + t_ij)
Start with E₁ = 0, move left to right

formulas.label: LATEST EVENT TIME (L)
formulas.text:
Backward pass: L_i = min(L_j − t_ij)
Start with L_n = E_n, move right to left

formulas.label: CRITICAL PATH
formulas.text:
Activities where E_i = L_i AND E_j = L_j AND E_j − E_i = t_ij
Total float = 0 on critical path

formulas.label: FLOATS
formulas.text:
Total Float (TF) = L_j − E_i − t_ij
Free Float (FF) = E_j − E_i − t_ij
Independent Float (IF) = E_j − L_i − t_ij
IF ≤ FF ≤ TF  always

cards.alert: Critical path = longest path through network = minimum project duration. Delaying any critical activity delays the whole project. TF = 0 for all critical activities.

cards.heading: PERT — THREE TIME ESTIMATES

formulas.label: THREE TIME ESTIMATES
formulas.text:
to = optimistic time (best case)
tm = most likely time (mode)
tp = pessimistic time (worst case)

formulas.label: EXPECTED TIME (te)
formulas.text:
te = (to + 4tm + tp) / 6
(Beta distribution assumed)

formulas.label: VARIANCE (σ²) & STD DEVIATION (σ)
formulas.text:
σ² = [(tp − to) / 6]²
σ = (tp − to) / 6

formulas.label: PROJECT PROBABILITY
formulas.text:
Z = (Ts − Te) / σ_cp
Ts = scheduled time, Te = expected time
σ_cp = √(Σσ²) along critical path
Use Z-table for probability

points: Variance is additive along a path (not standard deviation)
50% probability of completion at expected project duration
Higher Z → higher probability of completing within Ts

cards.heading: CRASHING — TIME-COST TRADE-OFF

formulas.label: COST SLOPE
formulas.text:
Cost slope = (Crash cost − Normal cost) / (Normal time − Crash time)
Crash the activity with LOWEST cost slope on critical path first

points: Normal point: normal time, normal cost
Crash point: minimum possible time (crash time), maximum cost
Direct cost increases as project duration decreases (crashing)
Indirect cost (overhead) decreases as project duration decreases
Optimum duration: minimum total cost = direct + indirect
Crash only critical activities — crashing non-critical has no effect on duration

cards.heading: EARTHWORK — KEY TERMS
points: Cutting (Excavation): removal of soil/rock above formation level
Filling (Embankment): compacted layers of soil below formation level
Free haul: distance within which earth can be moved at normal rate (usually 30 m)
Lead: horizontal distance of earth transportation
Lift: vertical height through which material is raised
Shrinkage factor: embankment volume > borrow pit volume (loose soil compacts)
Swell factor: cut volume > undisturbed volume (rock swells after blasting)

cards.heading: PRISMOIDAL FORMULA — EARTHWORK VOLUMES

formulas.label: TRAPEZOIDAL (Mean Area) METHOD
formulas.text:
V = L/2 × (A₁ + A₂)
A = (b + sh)h  where b=base, s=side slope, h=depth

formulas.label: PRISMOIDAL FORMULA (more accurate)
formulas.text:
V = L/6 × (A₁ + 4Am + A₂)
Am = mid-section area

formulas.label: PRISMOIDAL CORRECTION
formulas.text:
Cp = L/12 × (b₁−b₂)(h₁−h₂) × s
(subtract for cutting, add for filling)

cards.alert: Prismoidal formula is always more accurate than trapezoidal. UPPSC frequently asks which gives larger volume — trapezoidal gives higher volume for cutting.

cards.heading: COMPACTION — METHODS & EQUIPMENT
points: OMC (Optimum Moisture Content): moisture at which max dry density achieved

Modified Proctor: 4.9 kg rammer, 450 mm drop, 5 layers, 25 blows
Field compaction control: Sand replacement method (IS 2720 Part 28)
